food_subgroup: Pork dishes — deep-fried or roasted pork belly

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for crispy fried pork belly; closest parent 'pork belly food product' (FOODON_03302757) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Pork belly) is the closest parent; no specific code for crispy fried preparation exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226934003 represents pork belly as a food item; no specific crispy/fried preparation subtype available.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of crispy pork belly is approximately 200 g, consisting of several slices of deep-fried pork belly with crackling skin, often served with dipping sauce and rice or fresh herbs.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, belly (side), raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10006); adapted for deep-fried preparation using USDA cooking retention factors and comparable fried pork belly references
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Raw pork belly (USDA SR Legacy NDB 10006 / FDC ID 167903) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values adjusted upward for fat concentration and energy density following deep-frying, consistent with USDA Table of Cooking Yields for Meat and Poultry. Sodium elevated to reflect marinade (fish sauce, salt). Carbohydrate contribution is minimal from marinade spices. Final per-100g values represent a cooked, deep-fried pork belly product.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein- and fat-dominant foods: pork and analogous high-fat meat products — University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crispy fried pork belly. GI estimated at 27 based on analogous high-fat, low-carbohydrate pork products in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). High fat and protein content with minimal carbohydrate (1.2 g/100g) yields a very low GI consistent with other fatty pork preparations. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (27 × 1.2 × 2) / 100 = 0.65, rounded to 0.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg/100g) from fish sauce and salt marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g per meal and avoid additional high-sodium condiments, as a 200 g serving provides approximately 1040 mg sodium, representing nearly half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI ~27) but high saturated fat content (12.8 g/100g) which may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: While the low GI of crispy pork belly poses minimal acute glycemic risk, the high saturated fat content may contribute to insulin resistance over time; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume moderate portions (100–150 g) and pair with non-starchy vegetables rather than white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (35.5 g/100g), saturated fat (12.8 g/100g), cholesterol (~82 mg/100g), and sodium (~520 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Crispy pork belly is high in saturated fat and sodium, both of which are established cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict consumption to occasional small portions (≤100 g) and prioritise leaner protein sources as dietary staples.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Pork Belly (Vietnamese: Ba Rọi Chiên Giòn) is a deep-fried pork belly dish popular in Vietnamese cuisine and widely available in Singapore through Vietnamese restaurants. The dish features thick slices of pork belly — comprising alternating layers of lean meat, fat, and skin — that are marinated in a blend of fish sauce, garlic, lemongrass, five-spice powder, and salt before being deep-fried until the skin achieves a characteristic golden, crackling crispness. The interior remains tender and juicy while the exterior provides a satisfying crunch. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as part of a Vietnamese-style rice plate or as a sharing dish accompanied by fresh herbs, pickled vegetables, and a dipping sauce such as nước chấm. Nutritionally, crispy pork belly is energy-dense (~395 kcal/100g) and high in total fat (35.5 g/100g) and saturated fat (12.8 g/100g), making it a food that should be consumed in moderation. Sodium content is elevated (~520 mg/100g) due to the fish sauce marinade. The glycemic index is low (~27) owing to the negligible carbohydrate content (1.2 g/100g), meaning it does not cause rapid blood glucose spikes. However, the high saturated fat and sodium levels make it a concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free. It is not halal due to the use of pork.

Paired with steamed jasmine rice or broken rice

confidence_reason: No exact match for crispy fried pork belly (Ba Rọi Chiên Giòn) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw pork belly (FDC ID 167903) with cooking adjustments for deep-frying. GI is estimated from analogous high-fat pork products in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA anchor is a high-quality primary source and the adaptation methodology is well-documented.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw pork belly (FDC ID 167903) with upward fat and energy adjustments for deep-frying based on USDA cooking retention factors. Sodium is elevated to reflect fish sauce and salt marinade typical of Vietnamese-style preparation. 2. GI of 27 is an analogous estimate based on high-fat, low-carbohydrate pork products from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008); no direct GI measurement for this specific preparation was found. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork belly data and are not independently verified for this specific Vietnamese preparation. 4. The dish is served at Viet'Spice - Hương vị Việt (Circular Road, Singapore) as confirmed by field survey data. 5. The food is classified as Singapore cuisine in the input record; however, the dish is of Vietnamese origin (Ba Rọi Chiên Giòn) and is served in a Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore — multilingual naming therefore includes both Singaporean mandatory languages (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) and Vietnamese. 6. No -1 sentinel values are used for core macros; all secondary nutrient -1 values have been replaced with estimated numeric values from the USDA source. All numeric fields are populated.
